macro_segments.title: Introduction to Daf 63 and the Types of Menachot Vessels

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks on Daf 63 as the final daf of the perek on menachot
Overview of Hagasha and Tenufa; transition to specific menachot types

macro_segments.title: Mishna: The Machavat and Marcheshet—Flat Frying Pan vs. Deep Saucepan

micro_segments.title: Mishna text: One who vows to bring a mincha in a machavat or marcheshet
Distinction between vessels: machavat (flat, uncovered) vs. marcheshet (deep, with cover)
Rabbi Yose HaGelili's explanation: cover vs. no cover; symbolism of hidden vs. revealed
Gemara challenges the symbolism: could it be the reverse?
Rabbi Chanina ben Gamliel: deep vs. flat; how boiling/bubbling differs from frying

macro_segments.title: Gemara: Beit Shammai vs. Beit Hillel on the Names Marcheshet and Machavat

micro_segments.title: Baraita: Beit Shammai says if you vow on a marcheshet, it is undetermined until Eliyahu comes
Ambiguity of the vow: does marcheshet refer to the vessel or to a type of mincha?
Tosafot's discussion: could the vower clarify intent, or transfer kedusha?
Beit Hillel: marcheshet is a vessel name; one who says harei alai marcheshet donates a vessel
Description of the marcheshet as a deep vessel with indented spaces (like a muffin pan)

macro_segments.title: Mishna: Baked Mincha in an Oven—Tanur, Kupach, and Prohibited Vessels

micro_segments.title: Mishna: One who vows to bring a baked mincha in an oven
Prohibited vessels: kupach (small oven), tiles (re'afim), Arab pots (yorot ha'arviyim)
Assumption that the mincha is made at home and brought (not in the Mikdash)
Rabbi Yehuda: tanur mentioned twice to permit a kupach as a lesser oven
Rabbi Shimon: tanur twice means baked in an oven AND sanctified in an oven

macro_segments.title: Gemara: When Does the Mincha Acquire Kedusha? The Question of Yotzei (Taking Out)

micro_segments.title: Gemara challenges: if sanctified in the oven, how can it be taken out of the Mikdash (Yotzei problem)?
Broader context: mincha kedusha is different from animal korban; earlier sanctification stages
Tosafot's question: is it possible to take a mincha out after kedushat kli?
Rava's reinterpretation: sanctify the mincha specifically for ma'afeh tanur type

macro_segments.title: Mishna: Baked Mincha with Chalos and Rekikin—Half and Half Debate

micro_segments.title: Mishna and Baraita: one who vows to bring a baked mincha
Rabbi Yehuda: do not bring half loaves and half wafers (must choose one type)
Rabbi Shimon: permit mixing because it is one korban with two variations
Method of mixing: break into pieces, mix, and take a kometz from both types

macro_segments.title: Gemara: Rabbi Yose b'Rabbi Yehuda's Drasha from 'Kol Mincha'—Two Distinct Types

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yose b'Rabbi Yehuda quotes a different pasuk to support no mixing
Drasha: 'kol mincha' appears twice (baked and unbaked), each implying distinct types
Gemara: 'shemen shemen' twice in the pasuk shows two distinct options
Rabbi Shimon's response: without the double 'shemen,' would only require half and half
Gemara distinguishes Rabbi Yose from his father on b'diavad (after the fact)

macro_segments.title: Perek Rabbi Ishmael: The Omer—Introduction and Overview

micro_segments.title: New perek begins: Rabbi Ishmael on the Omer brought on the sixteenth of Nisan
Mishna: Rabbi Ishmael on how much grain to start with for an isaron on Shabbat vs. weekday

macro_segments.title: Mishna: Rabbi Chananya Segan HaKohanim on the Public Cutting of the Omer

micro_segments.title: On Shabbat: one person, one scythe, one basket (minimizing effort and visibility)
On a weekday: three people, three scythes, three baskets (public display)
Chachamim disagree: same procedure on Shabbat and weekday (three each)
Context: to refute the Sadducees; whether the Omer overrides Shabbat

macro_segments.title: Gemara: Why Different Amounts of Grain (Three vs. Five Se'im)?

micro_segments.title: Gemara questions Rabbi Ishmael's position: does he require a choice isaron from five se'im?
Rava explains: five se'im makes it easier (less sifting); three se'im requires more work
On weekday: use five se'im (more praiseworthy, less effort); on Shabbat: use three (minimize melachot)
Principle: better one melacha many times than many different melachot on Shabbat
Example: the five melachot to produce flour (cutting, winnowing, sorting, grinding, sifting)

macro_segments.title: Gemara: Comparing Rabbi Ishmael and Rabbi Shimon b'R. Yochanan—Minimizing Shabbat Violation

micro_segments.title: Gemara: Rabbi Ishmael and Rabbi Shimon b'R. Yochanan said essentially the same thing
Both limit effort/violation on Shabbat, but is it just effort or something deeper?
Example: on Pesach when 14th Nisan falls on Shabbat, Rabbi Shimon limits flaying of korban Pesach
Gemara nuances: perhaps Rabbi Ishmael limits flaying only because no religious purpose after chest exposed
Conversely, for the Omer, extra grain may be religiously warranted if it improves the mincha

topical_tags.term: Yehei munach ad sheyavo Eliyahu (put aside until Eliyahu comes)
